key_facts: Myers pleaded guilty to possessing an implement for counterfeiting state securities and transporting a stolen motor vehicle across state lines.
Myers still owes over $35,000 in restitution.
Over $30,500 has been deposited in Myers’s trust account, mostly from family and friends.
The government invoked the Mandatory Victims Restitution Act (MVRA) to turn over the accumulated deposits from family and friends to apply to Myers’s restitution obligation.
The district court granted the government’s motion to turn over the funds and apply them to Myers’s restitution obligation.
legal_issues: Whether the Mandatory Victims Restitution Act (MVRA) applies to the gradual accumulation of cash deposits from family and friends in an inmate’s trust account.
Whether the district court abused its discretion in declining to hold an evidentiary hearing on the composition of Myers’s trust account.
decision: The panel affirmed the district court’s decision to turn over the accumulated deposits from family and friends to apply to Myers’s restitution obligation. The panel rejected Myers’s argument that the MVRA only applies to one-time, lump-sum windfalls and held that it also applies to substantial aggregated sums from multiple sources. The panel also held that the district court did not abuse its discretion in declining to hold an evidentiary hearing on the composition of Myers’s trust account.
reasoning_summary: The panel majority held that the MVRA applies not just to one-time financial windfalls, but also to substantial aggregated sums from multiple sources, such as family and friends, that gradually accrue in an inmate’s trust account. The panel also held that the district court did not abuse its discretion in declining to hold an evidentiary hearing on the composition of Myers’s trust account.
